characters.blurb: Last king of Rome. After Lucretia’s death the people drove him out; he warred to return with Tuscan and Latin aid.
Led the expulsion of the Tarquins after Lucretia’s rape. As consul he executed his own sons for conspiring to restore the kings.
Co-consul with Brutus, but the Tarquin name made the people uneasy; he laid down office and went into exile.
Chaste wife of Collatinus; violated by Sextus Tarquinius, she killed herself before her kinsmen and so fired the revolt.
Present at his daughter’s death-oath; counted among the first men of the free Republic.
Publius Valerius won the people by lowering the fasces, building lower on the Velia, and passing appeal from the consuls.
Came to restore Tarquin, took the Janiculum, and tested Roman courage through Horatius, Mucius, and Cloelia before making peace.
Defended the Tiber bridge until it was cut behind him, then swam back in armor—Rome’s first great republican exemplar.
Entered the Tuscan camp to kill Porsenna, slew the wrong man, and thrust his hand into the fire to prove Roman resolve.
Given to Porsenna as hostage; she led the girls across the river, was honorably returned, and was rewarded with a horse.
Overheard the Vitellii and Aquillii plotting Tarquin’s return; his tip let Brutus catch the traitors—including Brutus’s sons.
Noble house caught plotting to restore the kings; exposed by Vindicius’s warning and their own letters.

characters.relation: Kin who plotted with the Aquillii
characters.blurb: Related to Brutus’s household; their royalist plot forced the liberator to judge his own sons.
Met Brutus in single combat in the first war of the Republic; both died on each other’s spears.

characters.relation: Valerian kinswoman in the hostage tale
characters.blurb: Named among the Roman women tied to Cloelia’s escape; the family’s popularity underwrites Valerius’s rise.
Sabine leader welcomed to Rome with his clients; his migration shows Poplicola’s open franchise.
Named among those who held the bridge beside Horatius before the final swim.
Plutarch glances at Domitian’s pride as the opposite of Poplicola’s voluntary lowering of consular show.

characters.relation: Consular colleague named in the wars
characters.blurb: Appears among early republican commanders in the Tuscan and Latin fighting after the kings.

characters.relation: Greek pair in the Parallel Lives
characters.blurb: Athenian lawgiver Plutarch sets beside Poplicola—both refused crowns and armed citizens with lawful appeal.

characters.relation: Consular colleagues in the early Republic’s wars
characters.blurb: Two Postumii serve under Poplicola: Tubertus as consul with Marcus Valerius, and Balbus as a night commander who closed the enemy’s ambuscade.

characters.relation: Citizen who urged moderation in the wealth dispute
characters.blurb: Private man who rose in the assembly to counsel Brutus and the Romans to keep Tarquin’s confiscated property rather than distribute it, setting the treasury question.

characters.relation: Dictator who built the second Capitoline temple
characters.blurb: Sulla rebuilt the Capitoline temple burnt in the civil wars, yet died before its dedication—the ironic reverse of Poplicola’s timely death before his house was demolished.

characters.relation: Emperor who built the third Capitoline temple
characters.blurb: Rebuilt the temple a third time after the Vitellian sedition; Plutarch notes he succeeded in his temples as in all his enterprises, unlike Sulla who died before dedication.
Chosen as Poplicola’s colleague in a second consulship; served while Poplicola led the armies against the Latin and Sabine threats after the Tarquin wars.
